variables.v: Speed of object moving in a circle (m/s)

locked_facts: Speed is constant, velocity direction changes continuously
Centripetal acceleration = v²/r, always directed toward center
Centripetal force is not a new force — it is the NET inward force
Centrifugal force does not exist in inertial frame
truth_statements: In uniform circular motion, the speed is constant but velocity changes due to continuous change in direction
The acceleration is directed toward the centre and is called centripetal acceleration
Centripetal force is not a new type of force — it is the name given to the net force directed toward the centre
The magnitude of centripetal acceleration is v²/r
There is no outward force on the body in circular motion when viewed from an inertial frame

aha_moment.what_to_show: Object moving on circle with velocity arrow tangential and acceleration arrow pointing inward to center — a 90° angle marker between them shown clearly at multiple positions
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: The 90° angle between velocity (tangential) and acceleration (radial inward) — labelled at every position around the circle
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see acceleration always perpendicular to velocity and always pointing inward — never outward, never tangential

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: Object moving along a circular path. Only the velocity arrow is shown, tangent to the circle. Speed readout is constant.
simulation_states.emphasis: Velocity direction keeps changing even though speed stays the same

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: Acceleration arrow appears, pointing from the object toward the center. At 3-4 positions around the circle, both vectors are shown simultaneously.
simulation_states.emphasis: Acceleration arrow always points inward toward the center — at every position on the circle

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: At every position, a 90° angle marker is drawn between the velocity and acceleration vectors. The angle is always exactly 90°. A label reads 'v ⊥ a always'.
simulation_states.emphasis: Velocity is tangential, acceleration is radial — they are always perpendicular. Acceleration does not point outward.

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive mode: adjust speed and radius. Centripetal acceleration value updates in real time. Acceleration arrow length changes with v²/r.
simulation_states.emphasis: Doubling speed quadruples centripetal acceleration (v² dependence). Increasing radius decreases acceleration.

common_misconceptions.belief: Centripetal acceleration points outward or is tangential to the circular path
common_misconceptions.correction: NCERT says the acceleration in uniform circular motion is directed toward the centre (centripetal). There is no outward force in an inertial frame.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show acceleration arrow always pointing inward to center with 90° angle marker between velocity and acceleration at every position

legend.meaning: Perpendicular — v and a are always at 90°

typical_question_types: minimum speed at top of vertical circle
common_traps: forgetting velocity is tangential not radial
using centrifugal force in inertial frame
